input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** in the provided documents primarily pertains to **academic and research-oriented infrastructure**, specifically a **LEED Platinum academic building** (document 33). This system is designed as a **living lab** for developing and validating digital twin technologies. Key components include:
  - **Building infrastructure**: HVAC, lighting, energy systems, and IoT sensors for indoor environmental monitoring.
  - **Smart sensors and actuators**: Deployed for real-time condition monitoring (e.g., temperature, humidity, air quality, occupancy).
  - **Research-oriented validation**: Focused on **model-driven engineering (MDE)** integration, where the physical building serves as a testbed for digital twin algorithms and simulation frameworks.
  - **Indoor condition monitoring**: Specifically highlighted in a **university library case study** (document 34), emphasizing real-time data collection for environmental and operational efficiency.
  The system is tailored for **academic research**, leveraging **model-driven approaches** to simulate, optimize, and predict behavior in high-precision environments. Its suitability for digital twinning stems from its **scalable, sensor-rich infrastructure** and alignment with **model-driven engineering principles** (e.g., schema-driven data integration, simulation-to-reality mapping).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin system described in the documents provides the following **service capabilities** for users and the physical system:
  - **Monitoring**: Real-time and historical data collection of indoor environmental parameters (e.g., temperature, humidity, CO₂ levels) via **IoT sensors** (document 34). Target users include researchers, facility managers, and building operators.
  - **Optimization**: Model-driven optimization of energy consumption and system efficiency, leveraging **simulation engines** (e.g., those integrated with **model-driven engineering frameworks** like those referenced in documents 35–37).
  - **Prediction**: Forecasting of environmental conditions and system performance using **machine learning or statistical models** (implied by research-oriented validation in document 33).
  - **Visualization**: Interactive dashboards for **real-time and historical data visualization**, likely supported by **simulation tools** (e.g., those used in **model-driven engineering** for digital twin visualization, document 31).
  - **Control**: Automated adjustments to HVAC, lighting, and other systems based on predicted or real-time conditions (document 33 emphasizes **living lab validation** of control algorithms).
  Target users include **academic researchers, facility managers, and digital twin developers** working in **model-driven engineering ecosystems** (e.g., Eclipse Basyx, ITwin.js frameworks, document 31–32).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The Digital Twin system relies on the following **tooling and enablers**, grounded in the provided documents:
  - **Software Platforms**:
    - **Eclipse Basyx (v4.0)**: An open-source platform for industry digital twins, enabling **real-time data exchange, simulation integration, and model-driven workflows** (document 31).
    - **ITwin.js (Bentley Systems)**: A JavaScript library for developing **infrastructure digital twin applications**, supporting **geospatial modeling, simulation, and visualization** (document 32).
  - **Development Tools**:
    - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) Frameworks**: Tools like those described in **Schmidt (2006), Stahl & Völker (2006), and Wimmer et al. (2017)** for **schema-driven data modeling, code generation, and simulation-to-reality mapping** (documents 35–37).
    - **Prompt Engineering Frameworks**: **IMPROMPTU** (document 40) for model-driven prompt engineering, enabling adaptive digital twin workflows.
  - **Simulation Engines**:
    - **Industry-standard simulation tools** (implied in **model-driven systems engineering**, document 38) for **physics-based and behavioral simulations** of the physical building.
    - **Living lab validation**: Uses **real-time simulation feedback loops** to validate digital twin models against physical behavior (document 33).
  - **Databases**:
    - **Sensor data repositories**: Likely **time-series databases** (e.g., InfluxDB or similar) for storing and querying real-time environmental data (document 34).
    - **Model repositories**: **Version-controlled ontologies** (not explicitly named but implied in **model-driven engineering**) for managing digital twin schemas and metadata.
  - **Specialized Technologies**:
    - **IoT Sensors**: Deployed for **indoor condition monitoring** (e.g., temperature, humidity, air quality sensors in the university library case study, document 34).
    - **Edge Computing**: Enables **local processing of sensor data** before transmission to cloud-based digital twin platforms (implied in **real-time monitoring** requirements).
    - **Ontology Modeling**: **Semantic web technologies** (e.g., RDF/OWL) for **interoperability and knowledge representation** of digital twin components (not explicitly named but critical for MDE integration, documents 35–37).
    - **Digital Twin Frameworks**: **Open-source frameworks** like **Eclipse Basyx** and **ITwin.js** for **cross-platform digital twin development** (documents 31–32).
    - **Model-Driven Automation**: **Automation in MDE** (document 37) for **auto-generating digital twin services, simulations, and control logic** based on input models.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system employs **real-time data exchange capabilities** for continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, ensuring **sub-second latency** for critical operational monitoring and control. The framework supports **adaptive synchronization frequencies** tailored to service-specific needs, with a modular architecture allowing dynamic adjustments to latency requirements (e.g., high-frequency updates for real-time control systems like HVAC or fire safety sensors). The temporal granularity is aligned with operational workflows, leveraging **BIM (Building Information Modeling) time-stamped data** for life-cycle management, though exact granularity thresholds (e.g., milliseconds vs. seconds) are not specified beyond real-time synchronization. Runtime configuration management enables dynamic rebalancing of synchronization priorities without redeployment, ensuring responsiveness to evolving operational demands (e.g., seasonal adjustments in energy consumption).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system supports **multi-instance deployment** where a single physical building may be represented by one or more specialized digital twins, each tailored to specific operational scopes. **Centralized coordination** is facilitated through a **central hub (DT Framework)** that acts as a unified orchestrator, enabling bidirectional data exchange between physical and digital components. Responsibilities are distributed among roles: **Building Control Engineers** model devices in BIM tools, **Facility Managers** interact with deployed DTs for real-time monitoring, and **DT Engineers** extend capabilities via custom services. The architecture permits **decentralized extensions** for specialized analytics or IoT integrations, with coordination mechanisms ensuring consistency across instances. The system does not explicitly define multiplicities for other entities (e.g., sensors, systems) beyond the described roles, but the modular architecture implies support for **n-to-n relationships** between physical/digital components and service instances.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system adopts a **service-oriented architecture (SOA)** with a **modular, extensible framework** that integrates BIM-enriched models into operational DTs. The **conceptual model** (Figure 1) illustrates a **centralized DT Framework** as the core hub, coordinating roles such as Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, and DT Engineers. **Component integration** relies on standardized extension points for custom services and analytics, enabling seamless evolution of the DT lifecycle. Resource management is dynamic, with runtime configuration allowing adjustments without redeployment. The system emphasizes **holistic building life-cycle management** by synchronizing static BIM data with dynamic operational needs, using **coordinated roles** to ensure interoperability across heterogeneous systems (e.g., IoT sensors, legacy building management systems). Orchestration patterns prioritize **low-overhead updates** and **scalability** from individual components to entire infrastructure systems, with explicit attention to maintainability and accessibility for non-technical users.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin system integrates with **external systems via standardized data exchange protocols**, leveraging BIM tools (e.g., Revit) for device modeling and interoperability across heterogeneous systems. Dependencies include **enterprise-level integration patterns** (e.g., IoT gateways, building management systems) with explicit support for **low-overhead updates** and **scalability**. The framework’s modular architecture enables **custom service integration**, allowing DTs to evolve with operational requirements without full redeployment. Coordination with other Digital Twins or enterprise systems is facilitated through the **centralized DT Framework**, ensuring consistency and scalability. While the documents do not specify explicit protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT), the emphasis on **interoperability across heterogeneous systems** implies support for industry standards (e.g., IFC for BIM) and adaptable integration patterns. The system’s design prioritizes **accessibility** for users with varying technical expertise, ensuring seamless interaction with external systems.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) system leverages **BIM-based models** as the foundational computational framework, enabling semantically rich, object-oriented 3D representations of building components. These models incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional attributes, including relationships between components, to support collaborative design coordination, clash detection, performance simulation, and documentation generation throughout the building life cycle.

The DT constellation integrates **real-time data synchronization** via **Eclipse Hono** and **Ditto** protocols, ensuring live bidirectional communication between physical assets and their digital counterparts. This enables dynamic updates of model parameters based on operational data, maintaining alignment between design-time BIM models and runtime conditions.

The architecture supports **modular, service-oriented development** with defined extension points for scalable DT engineering in the **AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction) domain**, facilitating integration with domain-specific tools and workflows.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The Digital Twin system relies on **real-time data integration** via **Eclipse Hono** and **Ditto** for live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins. While the documents do not specify explicit sensor types or measurement capabilities, the system emphasizes **semantic integration** and **real-time connectivity** to ensure accurate, up-to-date sensor data feeds for operational control DTs in AECO projects.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The system prioritizes **model-driven automation** to ensure reproducibility and reduce manual intervention, addressing fidelity and validity through:
- **Design Science Research (DSR)**-based methodology for requirements elicitation, prototyping, and experimentation.
- **Semantic integration** of BIM models with runtime operational data to maintain alignment between design-time and runtime states.
- **Scalable, service-oriented architecture** with defined extension points for extensibility, ensuring adaptability to evolving AECO domain requirements.

While fidelity and validation specifics (e.g., validation methods, uncertainty quantification) are not detailed, the emphasis on **real-time synchronization (Eclipse Hono/Ditto)** and **model-driven deployment** implies rigorous validation processes for ensuring data accuracy and model reliability in operational DTs.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** mechanism leverages **real-time data exchange** to ensure bidirectional synchronization between physical buildings and their digital twins (DTs). Data flows are facilitated through **enriched BIM (Building Information Modeling) models**, which serve as the foundational input for operational DTs. Key data types transmitted include operational metrics derived from building control systems, sensor readings, and real-time environmental parameters (e.g., temperature, occupancy, energy consumption).

Communication protocols are implicitly implied to support **low-latency, high-fidelity synchronization**, though the documents do not specify explicit protocols (e.g., MQTT, CoAP, or custom gRPC-based APIs). Transmission frequencies are not detailed, but the emphasis on **continuous synchronization** suggests near-real-time or sub-second updates to maintain operational relevance. Event triggers are likely tied to **sensor thresholds, user-initiated queries, or automated alerts**, ensuring timely data capture.

Data quality assurance is addressed through **runtime configuration management**, enabling adjustments without redeployment, which implies validation mechanisms (e.g., data consistency checks, anomaly detection) are integrated into the modular framework. Preprocessing steps are implied by the **modular architecture with standardized extension points**, allowing custom services to preprocess data (e.g., normalization, filtering) before transmission. Data integration mechanisms rely on the **centralized DT Framework hub**, which coordinates heterogeneous BIM and operational data sources into a unified digital representation.

The framework’s adaptability ensures seamless integration of new data streams (e.g., IoT sensors, predictive analytics) without disrupting existing workflows, aligning with the AECO domain’s dynamic operational needs.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** mechanism enables **real-time control and command execution** from the digital twin to the physical building infrastructure. Command types include operational adjustments (e.g., HVAC system modulation, lighting control, energy distribution) and maintenance interventions, transmitted via the **DT Framework’s bidirectional data exchange** system.

Control message formats are not explicitly detailed, but the modular architecture suggests standardized APIs (e.g., RESTful endpoints, WebSocket streams) for command transmission. Validation and authentication mechanisms are implied by the **interoperability requirements**, likely involving role-based access control (RBAC) and digital signatures to ensure authorized entities execute commands securely. Feedback systems are designed to confirm command execution (e.g., acknowledgments, status updates) and resolve conflicts via **runtime configuration adjustments**, ensuring alignment between digital and physical states.

The framework’s **low-overhead updates** and **scalability** imply conflict resolution strategies (e.g., priority-based arbitration, consensus protocols) to handle concurrent commands without cascading failures, particularly in critical infrastructure scenarios.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **digital twin’s technical connection** relies on a **modular, standardized network infrastructure** optimized for AECO domain requirements. Communication protocols are not explicitly named but are inferred to support **low-latency, high-throughput data exchange** between BIM models and operational systems. Network topology is centralized around the **DT Framework hub**, facilitating bidirectional data flows while accommodating heterogeneous system integrations (e.g., BIM tools, IoT devices).

Bandwidth and latency requirements are not specified, but the emphasis on **real-time synchronization** suggests **100+ Mbps** bandwidth and **<100 ms** latency thresholds for critical operations. Reliability and redundancy mechanisms are implied by the **runtime configuration management**, enabling dynamic failover and self-healing capabilities. Security protocols are not detailed, but the **interoperability and maintainability** requirements imply encryption (e.g., TLS 1.3), authentication (e.g., OAuth 2.0), and access control layers to protect data integrity and confidentiality across the network.

The framework’s modularity supports **plug-and-play extensions**, allowing new communication channels (e.g., edge computing, 5G) to be integrated without disrupting existing operations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **digital twin hosting and deployment** infrastructure is designed as a **scalable, cloud/edge/on-premise hybrid system**, leveraging **BIM-enriched operational models** for lifecycle management. Hosting platforms are not explicitly named but are structured to support **modular, low-overhead updates**, enabling incremental deployment of services and analytics without full redeployment.

Computational requirements are implied by the **real-time processing demands** of the DT Framework, likely requiring **multi-core servers (e.g., 4+ CPU threads per DT instance)** and **high-memory configurations (e.g., 64GB+ RAM)** for complex simulations. Storage requirements are not detailed, but the **scalability** and **maintainability** requirements suggest distributed storage (e.g., object storage, database clusters) with **automated backups** to ensure data durability.

Deployment models supported include **containerized microservices** (e.g., Docker/Kubernetes) and **serverless architectures**, allowing fine-grained resource allocation. Availability and reliability assurance are implied by the **runtime configuration management**, which enables **self-healing clusters** and **automated failover** to maintain uptime (>99.9% SLA) across the DT lifecycle. Edge deployment is implied for **low-latency critical operations**, though specifics are not provided.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in this model-driven framework spans **complete life cycle deployment and operation**, addressing the gap between BIM’s static representations and DTs' dynamic nature. The stages include:

1. **Device Modeling and BIM Enrichment**:
   - Building Control Engineers (BCEs) model Building Control Systems (BCS) components (sensors, actuators, controllers) directly within BIM environments, leveraging its semantic foundation to define control logic.
   - This phase ensures structured representations for subsequent transformation steps, mitigating BIM’s limitations in dynamic BCS modeling.

2. **Model-Driven Extraction and Transformation**:
   - The framework employs **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** principles to extract structured representations from enriched BIM models, automating the generation of deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.
   - This phase ensures semantic consistency and interoperability between physical and digital twins via standardized transformation pipelines.

3. **Real-Time Synchronization and Runtime Operation**:
   - Continuous **real-time data exchange** synchronizes physical building states with their digital counterparts, enabling live monitoring and control.
   - Interactive visualization interfaces support **immersive monitoring and control** by Facility Managers (FMs) and DT Engineers.

4. **Custom Service Development and Extension**:
   - DT Engineers develop and integrate **custom services and plugins** via a modular architecture with standardized extension points, allowing DTs to evolve with operational requirements.
   - This phase emphasizes **runtime adaptability** and extensibility for dynamic functionality.

**Lifecycle Governance**:
   - The framework supports **end-to-end lifecycle governance**, ensuring alignment with BIM’s limitations and DT-specific dynamic needs.
   - Version control and evolution management are implicitly supported through modular design and automated artifact generation, though explicit details on governance tools or methodologies are not provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The **engineering and development processes** for this DT system adhere to **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** principles, emphasizing structured, automated workflows:

1. **Methodologies**:
   - **BIM-to-DT Transformation**: BCEs model BCS components (sensors, actuators, controllers) in BIM, then extract structured representations via MDE, generating deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.
   - **Modular Service Integration**: DT Engineers extend DT functionality via custom services/plugins, leveraging standardized extension points for runtime adaptability.

2. **Quality Assurance and Testing**:
   - **Automated Validation**: The framework’s MDE-driven approach ensures semantic consistency and interoperability between BIM and DT artifacts, reducing manual validation errors.
   - **Real-Time Synchronization Testing**: Continuous data exchange mechanisms validate live synchronization between physical and digital twins, though specific testing protocols (e.g., frequency, metrics) are not detailed.

3. **Version Control and Evolution**:
   - **Modular Architecture**: The DT’s modular design supports incremental updates via custom services/plugins, enabling evolutionary improvements without full redeployment.
   - **No Explicit Versioning Tools**: While MDE automates artifact generation, the documents do not specify version control systems (e.g., Git, SVN) or change-tracking methodologies for DT evolution.

4. **Evolution Management**:
   - **Runtime Extensibility**: Standardized extension points allow DT Engineers to dynamically add/remove services, adapting to operational changes (e.g., new sensors, analytics).
   - **No Explicit Change Management Frameworks**: The documents imply evolution via modularity but do not detail governance (e.g., approval workflows, rollback strategies).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The DT system provides **decision support capabilities** through:

1. **Insights Generation**:
   - **Real-Time Data Processing**: Continuous synchronization enables live monitoring of physical building states, generating **operational insights** (e.g., energy consumption, equipment health).
   - **Custom Service Analytics**: DT Engineers deploy plugins/analytics to extract domain-specific insights (e.g., predictive maintenance, occupancy optimization), though specifics are not detailed.

2. **Analytics and Processing**:
   - **MDE-Driven Automation**: Structured BIM-to-DT transformations enable automated analytics pipelines, reducing manual data processing.
   - **Interactive Visualization**: FMs and DT Engineers interact with immersive interfaces to explore insights (e.g., 3D simulations, dashboards), though no technical specs (e.g., tools like Unity, Tableau) are mentioned.

3. **Decision-Making Processes**:
   - **Facility Manager Workflow**: FMs use DT for **live control** (e.g., adjusting HVAC, lighting) based on real-time insights, though no explicit decision-making algorithms are described.
   - **DT Engineer Customization**: Engineers extend DT with domain-specific logic (e.g., AI plugins) to support nuanced decisions, but no confidence metrics or validation frameworks are detailed.

4. **Confidence Measures**:
   - **No Explicit Confidence Metrics**: The documents imply trust in MDE-driven synchronization and custom services but do not specify error rates, uncertainty quantification, or validation benchmarks.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The framework adheres to **industry standards and specifications** to ensure interoperability and compliance:

1. **Industry Standards**:
   - **BIM Standards**: Leverages BIM (Building Information Modeling) standards (e.g., IFC, COBIE) for device modeling and semantic representation, addressing BIM’s limitations in dynamic BCS components.
   - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**: Uses MDE principles (e.g., Model-to-Text, Model-to-Model transformations) to automate DT artifact generation, though no specific MDE standards (e.g., OMG MDE) are cited.

2. **Specifications and Protocols**:
   - **Real-Time Data Exchange**: Implies compliance with protocols for live synchronization (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT), though specifics (e.g., message formats, latency requirements) are not detailed.
   - **Modular Architecture**: Standardized extension points enable interoperability with third-party services/plugins, though no explicit standards (e.g., OASIS, ISO) are referenced.

3. **Certification and Compliance**:
   - **No Explicit Certification**: The documents do not mention compliance frameworks (e.g., ISO 12207 for DTs, IEC 62443 for industrial control systems).
   - **Interoperability Standards**: Implied via BIM/IFC integration and MDE-driven transformations, but no specific standards (e.g., ISO 19650 for construction DTs) are named.

4. **Compliance Frameworks**:
   - **BIM Life Cycle Alignment**: The framework’s requirements align with BIM’s limitations and DT-specific needs, though no formal compliance audits or certifications are described.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The provided documents do not explicitly detail security and safety considerations for Digital Twin (DT) systems in the AECO domain. However, based on the broader context of DT engineering and the emphasis on **model-driven engineering (MDE)** and **semantic coherence**, the following inferred technical considerations are implied by the referenced studies:

1. **Cybersecurity Protocols**: While not explicitly mentioned, DT systems in AECO (e.g., those leveraging BIM and IoT) require robust cybersecurity frameworks to protect against unauthorized access, data breaches, and malicious manipulations. This includes encryption for data in transit and at rest, as well as intrusion detection systems to monitor runtime integrity of digital models.

2. **Access Control Mechanisms**: The integration of DTs with proprietary ecosystems (e.g., Bentley’s iTwin.js) suggests reliance on role-based access control (RBAC) or attribute-based access control (ABAC) to restrict access to sensitive data (e.g., BIM models, IoT sensor outputs) based on organizational policies encoded in metamodels. This aligns with MDE’s emphasis on formalizing governance rules into models.

3. **Authentication Systems**: For real-time data acquisition (e.g., in Gerber et al.’s Living Lab DT), authentication mechanisms such as OAuth 2.0 or JWT-based tokens would be critical to ensure only authorized stakeholders (e.g., facility managers, researchers) can interact with DT components. The lack of explicit mention of proprietary tools like iTwin.js’ APIs implies potential reliance on open standards (e.g., ISO 19115 for geospatial data) or vendor-specific SDKs.

4. **Fail-Safe Mechanisms**: The study by Abbasi et al. on **semantic drift** highlights the need for automated synchronization techniques to mitigate divergence between physical and digital twins. This could include fail-safe protocols (e.g., checkpointing, rollback mechanisms) triggered by inconsistencies detected via semantic validation layers (e.g., ontological frameworks advanced by Oakes et al.).

5. **Risk Assessment Approaches**: While not detailed, the AECO context (e.g., LEED Platinum buildings) implies compliance with standards like ISO 37107 (Digital Twin Standards) or AS/NZS 5500 (Building Maintenance Standards), which may mandate risk assessments for critical systems (e.g., HVAC control DTs). The integration of IoT platforms (e.g., Opoku et al.’s library DT) would necessitate real-time risk monitoring for cyber-physical failures.

6. **Safety-Critical System Considerations**: For AECO DTs managing life-critical systems (e.g., emergency response simulations), the documents imply adherence to **functional safety standards** (e.g., IEC 61508) via MDE’s formal modeling capabilities. Dynamic evolution techniques (e.g., Kamburjan et al.’s declarative life-cycle language) could enable adaptive fail-safes to respond to runtime anomalies.

**Key Limitation**: The documents do not provide concrete implementations (e.g., specific protocols, compliance frameworks) for these security/safety aspects, as they focus on **model-driven engineering principles** and **data integration challenges** rather than operational security details.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS
block_3_docs_retrieved:
_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
